summary:
The Sauveterrian culture was an early Mesolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of France and adjacent areas of western Europe, dated to approximately 9500–8000 BCE. Named after the site of Sauveterre-la-Lemance in the Lot-et-Garonne department of southwestern France, the Sauveterrian is defined by its distinctive geometric microlith industry — small, precisely shaped flint tools including triangles, trapezes, and crescents that were hafted in wood or bone handles to create composite weapons and tools.

Sauveterrian communities lived as mobile hunter-gatherers in the diverse postglacial forests and river valleys of western Europe, following the successional expansion of temperate woodland as the climate warmed after the Ice Age. Their sites, typically small campsites rather than large settlements, reflect seasonal mobility in pursuit of game, fish, and plant foods. The Sauveterrian forms part of the broader European Mesolithic cultural mosaic that persisted from approximately 10,000 to 5,500 BCE, when farming communities beginning to spread from southeastern Europe gradually displaced or absorbed these hunting-gathering traditions. The [[event:1280|Azilian culture]] of the preceding period provided some technological antecedents for the Sauveterrian microlithic industry.
